title: Psalms 121
subtitle: I Lift My Eyes to the Mountains

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Song of Ascent

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “My Help Comes from the LORD”

heb.gloss: to keep / to watch / to guard
heb.paragraph: "He who watches (shāmar) over Israel will neither slumber nor sleep" (v.4). The pilgrimage’s guardian psalm. The pilgrim looks up at the mountains and asks: where does my help come from? (v.1). Answer: from the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth (v.2). God will not let your foot slip (v.3). The word shāmar (keep/guard) appears six times in eight verses — the psalm’s keyword. God keeps watch without interruption: no slumber, no sleep.

notes.note: "I lift my eyes to the mountains — where does my help come from? My help comes from the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth." The question and answer. Mountains were where gods and bandits both lived; the pilgrim looks past both to the Creator.

"He will not let your foot slip — he who watches over you will not slumber; indeed, he who watches over Israel will neither slumber nor sleep." Four assurances: no slipping, no slumbering, no sleeping. God’s vigilance is perpetual.

refs.note: Our help is in the name of the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth.
God will never leave or forsake you; the Lord is my helper.
The Creator does not grow weary; he gives strength to the weary.

hist.context: Psalm 121 is perhaps the most beloved of the Ascent psalms, recited at Jewish funerals and Christian services alike. The pilgrim lifts eyes to the hills—not as the source of help (pagan high places) but asking where help comes from. The answer: from the LORD, maker of heaven and earth. This may be a dialogue between pilgrim and priest, with the priest assuring divine protection.

sections.header: Verses 5–8 — “The LORD Will Watch Over Your Coming and Going”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD will watch over your coming and going (ṣēʾṭ) both now and forevermore" (v.8). God is shade at your right hand (v.5). Sun won’t harm by day, nor moon by night (v.6). The LORD keeps you from all harm; he watches over your life (v.7). The scope: all harm, all comings, all goings, now and forevermore. The psalm covers every dimension: time (now–forever), space (coming–going), threat (sun–moon), and agency (God keeps).

notes.note: "The LORD watches over you; the LORD is your shade at your right hand. The sun will not harm you by day, nor the moon by night." Total environmental protection: daytime heat, nighttime cold. The Lord as shade and shield.

"The LORD will keep you from all harm — he will watch over your life. The LORD will watch over your coming and going both now and forevermore." The psalm’s grand finale: ALL harm, ALL movement, ALL time. Six times shāmar in eight verses. The most comprehensive protection promise in the Psalter.

refs.note: God shelters his people; the sun will not harm them, nor scorching heat.
The sun will not beat down on them; he who has compassion will guide them.
The LORD watch over you—the priestly blessing echoes the psalm's assurances.

hist.context: The five-fold 'watch over' (shamar) dominates the psalm's second half. God guards against sunstroke by day and moon-madness by night—covering all dangers, all times. The promise extends to 'coming and going' and 'forevermore,' transcending the pilgrimage to encompass all of life. This psalm became a traveler's prayer, recited when leaving home and entering new territory.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in I Lift My Eyes to the Mountains.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVthe Lord will watch over your coming and going both now and forevermore.ESVThe LORD will keep your going out and your coming in from this time forth and forevermore.NIVindeed, he who watches over Israel will neither slumber nor sleep.ESVBehold, he who keeps Israel will neither slumber nor sleep.

rec.text: ""I lift my eyes to the mountains — where does my help come from? My help comes from the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth." The question and answer. Mountains were where gods and bandits both lived;…"

rows.label: Coming and Going — 121:5–8
rows.text: "The LORD will watch over your COMING AND GOING, now and FOREVERMORE." Sun, moon, all harm — covered.

hebtext.gloss: to keep / to watch / to guard
hebtext.note: "He who watches (shāmar) over Israel will neither slumber nor sleep" (v.4). The pilgrimage’s guardian psalm. The pilgrim looks up at the mountains and asks: where does my help come from? (v.1). Answer: from the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth (v.2). God will not let your foot slip (v.3). The word shāmar (keep/guard) appears six times in eight verses — the psalm’s keyword. God keeps watch without interruption: no slumber, no sleep.

"The LORD will watch over your coming and going (ṣēʾṭ) both now and forevermore" (v.8). God is shade at your right hand (v.5). Sun won’t harm by day, nor moon by night (v.6). The LORD keeps you from all harm; he watches over your life (v.7). The scope: all harm, all comings, all goings, now and forevermore. The psalm covers every dimension: time (now–forever), space (coming–going), threat (sun–moon), and agency (God keeps).

themes.note: Faith peaks: the pilgrim’s trust psalm — six times "he watches." Sovereignty: the Maker of heaven and earth keeps every step. The most beloved Song of Ascent.
